params.text:
Who to ask when you are not sure

Nobody expects you to know everything about medicines, and pretending to is how harm happens. What is expected is that you ask the right person, quickly.

  • The community pharmacist: usually the most accessible medicines expert you have. Ask what a label instruction means, how something should be stored, or what to do about a supply problem.
  • The GP or prescriber: for whether a medicine is still right for the person, effects they are reporting, or a prescription that looks unexpected.
  • The district or community nurse: for clinical tasks and anything involving wounds, injections, catheters or specialised techniques.
  • NHS 111: for urgent advice that is not an emergency, such as a dose that may have been taken twice.
  • 999: for collapse, difficulty breathing, unresponsiveness or signs of a severe allergic reaction. Call first, tell your manager afterwards.
  • Your manager and the medicines policy: for what your service allows, and how to record and report anything that went wrong or nearly did.

params.alt: A care worker phoning a pharmacist for advice

params.text:
Competence is shown, not certificated

Finishing an e-learning course does not make you competent to administer medicines. A certificate records that you covered the knowledge. Competence is judged when someone qualified observes you doing the task correctly in real conditions and signs to say so.

  • Assessed, then reassessed: competency is checked through observed practice and rechecked periodically — sooner if a concern is raised or you have been away from the role.
  • Decline, escalate, record: if you are asked to do something you have not been trained and assessed for, say so and do not attempt it. Contact your manager or the relevant health professional, and write down what happened. Declining safely is correct, not obstructive.
  • Keep up when medicines change: after a hospital discharge or prescription change, check the current record and label against the care plan before you support anyone. Query anything that does not match rather than guessing.
  • Read, do not rely on memory: check the label and the patient information leaflet every time, however familiar the routine feels. Strengths and instructions change without the packet looking different.

params.question: Holding an up-to-date medicines e-learning certificate means you are competent to administer medicines to the people you support.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. A certificate on its own is not competence. Someone qualified must observe you carrying out the task correctly and sign it off, and that assessment has to be repeated periodically.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The certificate only evidences knowledge. Competence to administer requires assessed, observed practice signed off by an appropriate person, plus periodic reassessment, and the task must be recorded in the person's care plan.
